subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 13. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your job is to talk, show, and play alongside the child using the simple scripts and activities set out here. The child's own written work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Add two numbers to make a total up to 20 by counting on from the larger number.
Subtract a number by counting back from the starting number.
Use a number line to show each addition or subtraction as jumps.
Understand that counting on from the bigger number is quicker than counting everything from one.
Find how many are left when some are taken away.
Solve simple picture and story problems using add or take away.
Break a number to 20 into two parts (part-whole), e.g. 15 is 10 and 5.
Compare two numbers to 20 and say which is greater or fewer.
A handful of small countable objects: dates, dry beans, stones, bottle caps, or buttons (at least 20).

concepts.title: Adding by Counting On
concepts.explanation: Counting on is the first big strategy. Instead of counting every object from one, the child starts at the bigger number and counts forward by the smaller number. This is faster and builds real number sense. The key habit to teach is: start with the larger number, then count on the smaller one. How to teach it: Give the child 12 dates in one pile and 5 in another. Say, ‘You already have 12. We do not need to count those again. Let us hold 12 in our head and count on the 5 more.’ Touch the 5 dates one at a time…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Amina has 12 dates. Yusuf gives her 5 more. Let us start at 12.” You: “Put your finger on 12. Now count on: 13, 14, 15, 16, 17.” You: “Where did we land? 17! So 12 add 5 is 17. We did not…

concepts.title: Subtracting by Counting Back
concepts.explanation: Counting back is the mirror of counting on. To subtract, the child starts at the bigger number and counts backward by the amount being taken away. On the number line this is hopping to the left. The everyday meaning is ‘how many are left’ after some are used, eaten, or given away. How to teach it: Start with a real, concrete story. ‘You had 16 dates and you ate 4. How many are left?’ Put a finger on 16 and hop back four times: ‘15, 14, 13, 12.’ The child lands on 12. Say the number sentence together: ‘16 take away…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “There were 16 dates. Hassan ate 4. Put your finger on 16.” You: “Now count back: 15, 14, 13, 12. Which way are we hopping? Backward!” You: “We landed on 12. So 16 take away 4 leaves 12 dates.…

concepts.title: Using the Number Line
concepts.explanation: A number line is like a ruler for numbers — all the numbers in order, evenly spaced. It turns adding and subtracting into something the child can see and touch. For addition, start at the first number and jump forward; for subtraction, jump backward; and always land on the answer. How to teach it: Draw a clear line from 0 to 20 with every number marked, or chalk a giant one on the ground so the child can walk the jumps with their whole body. Physical movement — stepping forward five times, then backward two —…
concepts.example: Reading a friend's number line is a lovely thinking task. Show a line with a ring around 8 and four forward arrows landing on 12, and ask, ‘What calculation is this? What was added?’ Working backward from a picture to a…

concepts.title: Combining and Taking Away in Stories
concepts.explanation: Numbers come alive inside stories. Adding is combining two groups; subtracting is taking away or comparing. The skill here is helping the child hear a story, decide whether to add or take away, and then write a simple number sentence to solve it. How to teach it: Tell a short, familiar story and act it out with real objects. ‘There were 3 anjero on the plate. Mum added a stack of 10 more. How many now?’ Together decide: are we putting groups together (add) or taking some away (subtract)? Then choose counting on or…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “There were 14 goats in the pen. 5 goats wandered out. Add or take away?” Child: “Take away — they went out.” You: “Yes! Start at 14, count back 5.” You: “13, 12, 11, 10, 9. Nine goats left.…

concepts.title: Part-Whole and Comparing to 20
concepts.explanation: A part-whole picture shows how a whole number splits into two parts. This deepens the child's sense of how numbers combine. For example, 15 can be 10 and 5, or 8 and 7, or 12 and 3. There is no single right split — many pairs make the same whole, and discovering this is genuinely exciting for a young child. How to teach it: Put 15 stones in one bowl, then share them into two smaller bowls in different ways, counting each part. Record each way as a number sentence: 15 = 10 + 5, 15 = 8 + 7. Splitting a teen number…
concepts.example: Comparing numbers to 20 comes naturally from the number line: whichever number is further along the line is greater, and the one nearer the start is fewer. Lay two amounts side by side, match them into pairs, and the…

concepts.title: Key idea 1
concepts.explanation: Counting the wrong direction on the number line. The child hops forward when subtracting or backward when adding. Say the rule aloud every time: forward to add, backward to take away, and let them walk it with their body.

activities.title: Practise together
activities.materials: Paper, pencil and familiar household objects
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

questions.question: What does “Add / plus” mean?
options: Put two amounts together to find the total
Start at a number and count forward
Remove some to find how many are left
Start at a number and count backward
questions.answer: Put two amounts together to find the total
questions.hint: Use the Unit 13 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Add / plus means Put two amounts together to find the total.

A row of numbers in order to help counting

questions.explanation: Count on means Start at a number and count forward.

selfAssessment: I can add two numbers to make a total up to 20 by counting on from the larger number.
I can subtract a number by counting back from the starting number.
I can use a number line to show each addition or subtraction as jumps.
I can understand that counting on from the bigger number is quicker than counting everything from one.
I can find how many are left when some are taken away.
I can solve simple picture and story problems using add or take away.
I can break a number to 20 into two parts (part-whole), e.g. 15 is 10 and 5.
I can compare two numbers to 20 and say which is greater or fewer.
